title: Could Donald Trump end Iran's internet blackout?

transcript.text: Could President Trump end Iran's
internet blackout? The country is facing
some of the biggest protests it has ever
seen? In response, the government has
imposed a countrywide internet blackout.
And now Trump is threatening to get
involved.
>> We may get the internet going if that's
possible.
>> We may speak to Elon.
>> Iran's crackdown on the protests has
been brutal. One human rights group
confirms more than 650 people have been
killed, but some estimates are in the
thousands.
The protests, which began as a strike by
shopkeepers due to economic hardship,
have snowballed into a profound crisis
of legitimacy for the regime. It's not
the first time that Iran's leaders have
turned off the internet. But the fact
that this blackout is now in its sixth
day shows how nervous the regime has
become, especially because the blackout
imposes big costs on Iran's struggling
economy.
President Trump says he wants to rescue
the protesters.
>> The military is looking at it and uh
we're looking at some very strong
options.
>> But a large-scale military intervention
seems unlikely.
America doesn't have its usual military
strength in the region. For example,
none of its aircraft carriers are
deployed there.
One option could be for America to flood
Iran with Elon Musk's Starlink
terminals. These would allow more
Iranians to bypass the internet
shutdown. They work by connecting to a
constellation of low Earth satellites,
bypassing domestic telecommunications
infrastructure.
They're illegal in Iran, but tens of
thousands are thought to have been snuck
in since the protests in 2022.
Although the regime is reportedly
jamming Starlink terminals, this can
only be done at short range, which means
some protesters are able to record
events and upload the footage from safer
locations.
There are well-worn smuggling routes on
the Turkish border if Trump wanted to
get more in.
This is a dangerous moment for Iran's
leaders.
Around 30% of Iranians now live in
poverty, and there are shortages of
electricity, water, and food.
Confidence in the power of the Supreme
Leader, 86-year-old Ayatah Ali K has
faded. But as his government resorts to
violence, he shows no signs of wanting
to step aside.
Starlink provided an invaluable lifeline
to Ukraine's army at the start of the
Russian invasion. It's possible it could
do the same for Iran's protesters.
